Name: layerwise_bit_allocation_rate_distortion
Title: Optimal Bit Allocation Across Neural Network Layers via Rate-Distortion Theory
Short Hypothesis: Allocating bits across neural network layers based on their task-relevant information content minimizes task-relevant distortion, leading to more efficient compression.
Related Work: Existing works like Isik et al. (2021) and Bu et al. (2019) explore model compression using rate-distortion theory but do not address layer-wise bit allocation. Our proposal fills this gap by introducing a principled framework for distributing bits across layers based on mutual information and task-relevant distortion.
Abstract: Model compression is a critical technique for deploying neural networks in resource-constrained environments. While existing methods focus on overall compression ratios, they often overlook the varying contributions of different layers to the task. We propose a novel approach to optimize bit allocation across layers using rate-distortion theory and mutual information. Our method quantifies the task-relevant information in each layer and allocates bits to minimize task-relevant distortion. We derive theoretical bounds on the achievable compression rates and validate our approach through experiments on benchmark datasets. Results show that layer-wise bit allocation outperforms uniform compression, achieving higher accuracy at lower bit rates. This work bridges information theory with practical model compression, providing a principled framework for efficient neural network deployment.
Experiments: Evaluate mutual information between each layer and the task output using InfoNCE-style estimators.
Derive rate-distortion bounds for each layer based on mutual information and task-relevant distortion.
Implement layer-wise bit allocation using quantization and pruning, guided by the derived bounds.
Compare performance with uniform compression baselines on CIFAR-10, ImageNet, and MNIST datasets.
Metrics: Task accuracy, compression ratio, and task-relevant distortion (e.g., KL divergence between original and compressed model outputs).
Risk Factors and Limitations: Estimating mutual information accurately may be challenging in high-dimensional spaces.
The method assumes task-relevant information can be quantified, which may not hold for all tasks.
Layer-wise bit allocation may increase implementation complexity compared to uniform compression.
Theoretical bounds depend on assumptions about the source distribution, which may not always hold in practice.
